title: N-Queens
code: var diag = new HashSet<int>();   // r - c
var anti = new HashSet<int>();   // r + c
if (cols.Contains(c) || diag.Contains(r - c) || anti.Contains(r + c)) continue;
cols.Add(c); diag.Add(r - c); anti.Add(r + c); queenCol[r] = c;

board.label: 4x4 board — place one queen per row

frames.caption: Place one queen per row on a 4x4 board. Track attacked columns and both diagonals in sets so each safety check is O(1). n=4 has 2 solutions; we find the first.
frames.note: N-Queens is the showcase backtracking problem: the board is the state, "no two queens attack" is the constraint, and the conflict sets are the pruning. One queen per row reduces the search to "pick a safe column for each row," and the diagonal keys r-c / r+c make each safety test three constant-time lookups.

frames.caption: Row 0, col 0: SAFE (column, r-c=0, r+c=0 all free) — CHOOSE it. Add the three keys to the sets and recurse into row 1.
frames.note: CHOOSE: the square is safe, so place the queen and record its claims — add c to cols, r-c to diag, r+c to anti, and remember queenCol[r]=c. Then EXPLORE by recursing into the next row. Those three set inserts are exactly what makes future rows reject any square this queen attacks.

frames.caption: Row 1, cols 0..1 are ATTACKED (column 0 taken; ...). Skip — prune these branches.
frames.note: This square is attacked, so we skip it without descending — that prune kills the whole subtree below it. The diagonal trick is the elegant part: every cell on one "\" diagonal shares a constant r-c, and every cell on one "/" diagonal shares a constant r+c, so a queen at (qr,qc) attacks (r,c) along a diagonal exactly when r-c or r+c matches. Three hash sets turn "is this square attacked?" into three constant-time membership tests instead of re-scanning the board.

frames.caption: Row 1, col 2: SAFE (column, r-c=-1, r+c=3 all free) — CHOOSE it. Add the three keys to the sets and recurse into row 2.
frames.note: CHOOSE: the square is safe, so place the queen and record its claims — add c to cols, r-c to diag, r+c to anti, and remember queenCol[r]=c. Then EXPLORE by recursing into the next row. Those three set inserts are exactly what makes future rows reject any square this queen attacks.

frames.caption: Row 2, cols 0..3 are ATTACKED (column 0 taken; ...). Skip — prune these branches.
frames.note: This square is attacked, so we skip it without descending — that prune kills the whole subtree below it. The diagonal trick is the elegant part: every cell on one "\" diagonal shares a constant r-c, and every cell on one "/" diagonal shares a constant r+c, so a queen at (qr,qc) attacks (r,c) along a diagonal exactly when r-c or r+c matches. Three hash sets turn "is this square attacked?" into three constant-time membership tests instead of re-scanning the board.

frames.caption: UN-CHOOSE (1,2): row 2 had no safe column, so remove this queen and its set keys; try the next column in row 1.
frames.note: Row r ran out of safe columns — every square is attacked by a queen above. The partial board is a dead end, so we UN-CHOOSE the queen we placed in the previous row (remove its c, r-c, r+c from the sets) and resume scanning that row from the next column. This rewind is what makes it backtracking.

frames.caption: Row 1, col 3: SAFE (column, r-c=-2, r+c=4 all free) — CHOOSE it. Add the three keys to the sets and recurse into row 2.

frames.note: CHOOSE: the square is safe, so place the queen and record its claims — add c to cols, r-c to diag, r+c to anti, and remember queenCol[r]=c. Then EXPLORE by recursing into the next row. Those three set inserts are exactly what makes future rows reject any square this queen attacks.

frames.caption: Row 3, col 2: SAFE (column, r-c=1, r+c=5 all free) — CHOOSE it. Add the three keys to the sets and recurse into row 4.
frames.note: CHOOSE: the square is safe, so place the queen and record its claims — add c to cols, r-c to diag, r+c to anti, and remember queenCol[r]=c. Then EXPLORE by recursing into the next row. Those three set inserts are exactly what makes future rows reject any square this queen attacks.

frames.caption: r == 4: all 4 rows filled — first solution found! Queens at (0,1) (1,3) (2,0) (3,2).
frames.note: r == n: every row holds a non-attacking queen, so this is a complete solution. We would normally record a COPY of the board and keep searching for the other arrangements (n=4 has 2), but here we stop at the first. No two queens share a row (one per row), a column (cols set), or a diagonal (diag/anti sets) — the three sets enforced all of that incrementally.

frames.caption: Solved: queens at (0,1) (1,3) (2,0) (3,2). One per row, no shared column or diagonal — verified by the three conflict sets.
frames.note: Done. The first n=4 solution places queens at (0,1),(1,3),(2,0),(3,2). Backtracking explored only a sliver of the 4^4 placements because the diagonal/column sets pruned every attacked square the instant it was tried. Worst-case time is roughly O(n!) (the column branching shrinks as conflicts accumulate), and space is O(n) for the three sets plus the recursion stack.
